assessor_raw:
## Baseline Assessment — eval_scenario_606, Run 3

This experiment tests whether a transformer sequence model outperforms logistic regression on tabular features for 30-day hospital readmission prediction.

The temporal split is sound (training months 1–36, validation months 37–42, test months 43–48, no patient overlap). Preprocessing is correctly scoped to training data. Both models receive equal tuning effort. The evaluation metrics plan (AUROC + PR-AUC + calibration + net benefit) is comprehensive.

The primary methodological concern is the cohort restriction in Section 1: requiring ≥12 months of prior history before including a patient in the analysis. This restriction creates an unrepresentative evaluation cohort biased toward established, long-tenured patients with complete EHR records. The transformer's advantage over aggregate-feature logistic regression is maximized precisely when sequences are long and rich — which the restriction guarantees for all evaluation patients. Patients with shorter histories (new patients, recently insured individuals, those entering care late) are excluded from the evaluation even though a deployed system must serve them.

The scope disclaimer in Section 8 acknowledges bounded inference, but does not quantify the degree to which the restriction differentially advantages the transformer, making it impossible to know how much of any observed gap is cohort-selection-driven.

Verdict: **critique_wins** — the cohort restriction is a methodological flaw that biases the comparison in favor of the transformer beyond what the design can detect or bound.
